I enjoy the craft of software development and appreciate how much AI has unlocked creativity for me.

I approach problems through a systems thinking lens, looking at how the socio-technical parts interact. I know that "all models are wrong," but I'm constantly refining mine, for work and life. That shows up in how I lead: I try to make sure decisions are applied consistently and explained clearly, even when flexibility is warranted.

I believe building software is a team sport where you need to coach muscles and instincts. You need to practice discipline, set clear expecations about different roles, and constantly focus the team around a shared goal. Specifically, I have found it effective to create a repeatable mantra about what we are trying to deliever in the near term. When you ask anyone on the team, they should be able to tell it to you. 

When I'm not building software, I like to play board games, learn things (currently an improv student a [Second City](https://www.secondcity.com/chicago)), and be a father. 

Feel free to explore my [engineering playbook](/playbook), [projects](/projects), or [resume](/resume). You can also find me on [GitHub](https://github.com/danlafeir) and [LinkedIn](https://linkedin.com/in/danlafeir).

---

I think a lot about ideas of [paint drip people](https://tidyfirst.substack.com/p/paint-drip-people) and the [engineer/manager pendulum](https://charity.wtf/2017/05/11/the-engineer-manager-pendulum/).

Things I've built or contributed to.
Peer-to-peer CLI tool for encrypted text chat and voice calls.
CLI tool for engineering managers to generate JIRA agile metrics reports — cycle time analysis, throughput tracking, and Monte Carlo forecasting.
CLI tool that automates weekly timecard submission via the Tempo API, with macOS keychain integration for secure credential storage.
Pluggable local development toolkit that codifies routine logic execution while experimenting with AI agentic code capabilities.
Shared Go library for building CLI tools — plugin discovery, YAML config, OS-native secrets (macOS Keychain / Linux Secret Service), and GitHub release self-updating.
This site — a personal website built with Astro and deployed to GitHub Pages.

I am an engineering leader who has focused his career around idea of the Engineer/Manager Pendulum. I bring a range of experience from hands-on development and team management roles across diverse technical domains and organizational structures. My technical leadership style is centered around systems thinking, lean value-aligned software delivery, and partnership with business stakeholders. I have led through organizational change, harvested my learning over the years into reusable tools, and prioritize working software over "working" slideware.

Currently looking to swing back into an IC role, ideally in a more complicated, technical domain. I enjoy management and creating space for people to be successful but feeling that tension to get back into the craftsmanship
8 years leading teams — currently managing 22 people, 2 years managing managers
Technical domains: ReactNative (1.5 yrs), Search (2 yrs), Web GUIs (5 yrs), Infrastructure Platforms (5 yrs), SpringBoot Microservices (6 yrs), Data Engineering (3 yrs)
Leading three teams and one manager building systems in the Order domain — search of order data, asynchronous order workflows, payment capabilities, and analytical data products.
Built up 3 leaner, higher-functioning teams with some tough performance management, celebrated promotions and hiring people with the right aptitude and attitude
Built and executed a release strategy with Product to deliver net-new Order software starting with ~6 orders/day for select customers to create a faster feedback loop and surface unknown unknowns.

Facilitated bi-weekly prioritization meetings with partner SAP teams, who claimed to have no capacity for work, to balance work across new Order software delivery and a multi-year, massive SAP upgrade.

Delivered 4 Order Data Products for multiple analytical use cases, including research on frequency of orders with damaged products and inconsistent application of customer order settings across systems.
Drove cross-team kickoffs and working sessions with EMs and Product managers to break down work using domain-driven design and minimize friction in the team topology.
Senior IC role rotating across teams to solve specific, time-bound problems identified with the senior director of engineering.
Led strategic initiative to resolve critical legacy database performance issues by implementing a data retention policy that removed 500M+ records and stopped weekly production incidents.
Mentored and coached a senior iOS engineer into a technical lead role on a mobile team.
Reduced product delivery timeline for an inventory allocation HTTP API from 4 months to 1 month by partnering with Product and EM to apply lean engineering principles.
Developed a CLI tool for capturing capitalizable engineering time, improving financial reporting accuracy and reducing weekly timecard effort for ~50 engineers.
Evaluated on-call tooling and partnered with Datadog Product Management to support a director of engineering in purchasing Datadog On-Call and building a framework to scale incident management.
Rotated between technical lead, technical account manager, director of engineering, and senior IC roles depending on client and project need.
Built a Solr-based search platform enabling multiple legal domains to make their content searchable; used DCG with a curated corpus to assert non-negative performance impact per commit.
Partnered with VP of Engineering and Directors to scale engineering sensible defaults and deliver 2 board-visible projects to make customer data more usable for sellers.
Standardized the performance review process for ~55 engineers to solve feedback loop problems for consultants distributed across a large client on many different teams.
Implemented a platform engineering strategy — CLI tool, API-first tooling, Kubernetes, and starter-kits — that reduced a greenfield project's time-to-production from ~4 weeks to ~2–3 days.
Rotated between technical lead, engineering manager, and senior IC roles.
Led a development team of 7 engineers to deliver Manheim Express in 6 months, generating ~$500,000 in revenue and unlocking a new channel for the client's customers.
Presented architectural recommendations to C-level banking executives to decompose a legacy monolithic deployment tool and remove a major source of delivery friction.
Built a library enabling feature toggling in the browser via HttpOnly cookies to support delivery of a modernized React e-commerce experience.
IC across multiple projects, primarily GUI applications.
Contributed to an open source library for rendering images in React Native.
Led a JavaScript Frontend forum that evolved into an organization-wide technical community.
Presented an innovative ReactJS testing strategy centered on testing components as the unit under test.
IC across 2 client engagements, primarily GUI applications. Built a Vanilla JS interactive experience for the New York Philharmonic's Lincoln Portrait archive.

Confident writing JavaScript (TypeScript preferred), Go, Python, and Kotlin/Java with popular associated frameworks. Proficient with AI tools and prompt engineering. Experienced standing up Kubernetes clusters, CI/CD pipelines, and AWS infrastructure using Terraform and other IaC tools.

Ideas for career paths that decouples compensation from titles, supports multiple paths, and a structural engineering pendulum

![career progression](/images/playbook/career_progression.png)

*Here is an example of the what a breakdown of career progression*

## Principles
*These are the principles I would attempt to bring to an organization*

1. **Enhanced segmentation in levels** creates more advancement opportunities without inflating titles.
2. **Wider salary ranges for senior leadership roles** ranges widen at higher levels rather than requiring a management track to grow compensation.
3. **The Engineering Pendulum is supported by the salary structure** moving between IC and management shouldn't require a financial sacrifice.
4. **Architecture is a competency, not a role** the ability to reason architecturally should be developed across all levels, not gatekept behind a title. 
5. **Engineering individual contributors should be peers engineering management at different levels** creating healther, honest feedback loops about the technical quality of the system.
6. **There should be paths to rotate between Manager and Individual Contributor** rotation is important and both experiences are required to progress to senior leadership.

## Compensation Structure

- **Base pay** follows a logarithmic progression — meaningful early jumps that taper at senior levels.
- **Bonus** is tied to company performance with linear growth across levels.
- **Equity** increases inversely to base pay — higher-level engineers take more of their compensation in equity.

## Resources

- [The Engineer/Manager Pendulum](https://charity.wtf/2017/05/11/the-engineer-manager-pendulum/) — Charity Majors on career flexibility between IC and management tracks.
- [An Elegant Puzzle](https://lethain.com/elegant-puzzle/) — Will Larson on engineering management and organizational design.
- [Architects, Anti-Patterns, and Organizational Fuckery](https://charity.wtf/2023/03/09/architects-anti-patterns-and-organizational-fuckery/)

A progression framework for software engineers centered on knowing your "One Job" before expanding scope.

The framework is built around one core principle: [know your one job and do it first](https://charity.wtf/2021/03/07/know-your-one-job-and-do-it-first/). Master your current level before reaching for the next. Scope of impact grows with each level — from yourself, to your pair, to your team, to the organization, and beyond.

## L1 — Curious IC

You're building your foundation. The primary signals are **aptitude and attitude** — are you learning, asking good questions, and validating your assumptions with peers? At this level, it's more important to be curious and coachable than to be right.

- **Engineering competency:** You're learning the fundamentals — your team's tech stack, version control, and testing practices; you ask questions before making assumptions and validate your understanding with peers.
- **Influence:** Your scope is yourself; you earn trust through reliability — doing what you said you'd do.
- **Mentorship/coaching:** You seek out feedback actively and act on it; you're the one being coached.
- **Impact:** Your work is scoped to individual tasks; you complete them reliably and surface blockers early rather than going silent.
- **Communication:** You ask good questions, check your understanding before proceeding, and keep your pair updated on where you are.

## L2 — Strong IC

You deliver quality work independently. The team can hand you a problem and trust you to see it through.

- **Engineering competency:** You write clean, well-tested code without supervision; you practice TDD and hold yourself to it; you rotate pairs without missing a beat.
- **Influence:** Your influence is your own delivery; teammates trust you to own work end-to-end without hand-holding.
- **Mentorship/coaching:** You give written feedback to peers — specific, actionable, and timely; you're beginning to help newer engineers find their footing.
- **Impact:** You deliver reliably and are aligned with the team mission; you can articulate why the work matters, not just what it does.
- **Communication:** You communicate clearly about blockers and keep the team updated without being asked; you can explain your technical decisions to teammates.

## L3 — Strong IC with Team Impact

Your influence extends beyond your own delivery. You're shaping how the team works.

- **Engineering competency:** You refactor code to apply technical principles at scale; you identify architectural problems before they become production incidents.
- **Influence:** You shape how the team approaches problems; teammates adopt practices you've introduced because they work.
- **Mentorship/coaching:** You educate teammates on technical principles through code review and pairing; newer engineers on your team improve because of your engagement.
- **Impact:** Your self-directed improvements benefit the whole team; you balance assigned work with proactive investments that raise the team's floor.
- **Communication:** You facilitate meetings that produce clear, actionable outcomes; you can explain technical tradeoffs to the team without needing to defer to others.

## L4 — Exemplary IC amplifying the team

Your scope is the organization. You operate in ambiguous, high-stakes areas and make others better.

- **Engineering competency:** You own solution architecture end-to-end; you're the person teams call for genuinely hard technical problems; your architectural decisions account for the broader business context, not just the technical tradeoffs.
- **Influence:** You operate across the organization; your presence on a problem raises the quality of the outcome even when you're not the one writing the code.
- **Mentorship/coaching:** You mentor team members intentionally; you scale your knowledge through documentation and tooling, not just conversations.
- **Impact:** Your work removes organizational bottlenecks; you drive iterative delivery on complex, multi-stakeholder problems.
- **Communication:** You articulate architectural changes within the broader business context and earn stakeholder buy-in — not just team approval.

## L5 — Technical leader on the team, influencing peer teams

Your scope reaches beyond your team. You're the person peer teams seek out before making technical decisions that touch yours.

- **Engineering competency:** Your code and reviews set the quality ceiling for your team; you design solutions that work cleanly across team boundaries and flag where system seams create risk before they become incidents.
- **Influence:** Adjacent teams factor your technical opinions into their decisions; you're consulted, not just informed.
- **Mentorship/coaching:** You're actively developing 1–2 engineers toward L4; you can describe exactly what each one needs and why.
- **Impact:** You connect your team's work to the broader product roadmap; your changes improve systems your team doesn't own — engineers you've never worked with encounter better code because of decisions you made.
- **Communication:** You write design docs that adjacent teams actually use; you can represent technical tradeoffs clearly to non-engineers on your own team.

## L6 — Technical leader on the team, amplifying peer teams

You don't just influence how peer teams work — you actively make them better. Your patterns and practices spread because they're genuinely useful.

- **Engineering competency:** You identify and remove systemic quality problems that no single team owned; your architectural patterns are adopted across teams; you've simplified something significant, not just added to it.
- **Influence:** Peer teams change how they work based on your input; your voice carries weight in cross-team technical decisions without requiring a formal role.
- **Mentorship/coaching:** You develop engineers across teams, not just your own; you've helped someone reach L4 or materially accelerated a peer team engineer's growth.
- **Impact:** Your changes measurably reduce friction for multiple teams; you translate multi-team priorities into a coherent technical roadmap and anticipate cross-team conflicts before they surface as blockers.
- **Communication:** You write documents that change how teams think, not just what they do; you communicate proactively upward about cross-team technical health.

## L7 — Technical leader for a domain, influencing the system architecture

Your scope is a technical domain. You understand the full shape of the system and are shaping its next generation of architectural decisions.

- **Engineering competency:** Your reviews improve engineers' mental models, not just the PR; you can articulate where the system is healthy and where it's fragile across team boundaries; you're shaping the next major architectural decisions.
- **Influence:** Engineering leads across the org bring you into their hardest technical discussions; your judgment on system design carries weight with senior leadership.
- **Mentorship/coaching:** You're explicitly developing engineers toward L5–L6; you have a clear point of view on each person's path and what they need to get there.
- **Impact:** You hold a 12–18 month technical vision for your domain connected to company priorities; your decisions set constraints for other teams — a wrong call at your level takes multiple quarters to unwind.
- **Communication:** You make the case for major technical investments to non-technical stakeholders; your written thinking shapes org-wide decisions.

## L8 — Technical leader for a domain, amplifying the system architecture

You don't just influence the system architecture — you've moved it. You've driven significant change and made it stick.

- **Engineering competency:** The quality bar you set is the quality bar for your domain; you've navigated the org dynamics required to drive architectural change across teams and made it durable.
- **Influence:** Other technical leaders defer to your judgment on the hardest system-wide decisions; you shape technical culture, not just technical decisions.
- **Mentorship/coaching:** You've grown engineers to L7; you actively build the technical leadership bench for your domain and can describe the pipeline explicitly.
- **Impact:** You own the multi-year technical roadmap for your domain and hold it through org changes and shifting priorities; your work outlasts any particular team or product cycle — you've changed what "good architecture" means at your company.
- **Communication:** You represent the technical state of your domain to the executive team; your writing and speaking shape how the company thinks about technical quality.

## L9 — Technical leader across the organization, influencing the organizational architecture

Your scope is the organization. You see where the structure of teams and systems creates constraints on what the company can build — and you're making the case to fix it.

- **Engineering competency:** Your judgment on technical debt informs how the company allocates engineering investment; you see where org structure and system architecture are misaligned and make the case to resolve it.
- **Influence:** Your input changes how engineering teams are structured and resourced; executives adjust decisions based on your technical judgment.
- **Mentorship/coaching:** You're developing L7–L8 engineers; your investment in technical leadership has compounding returns across the org.
- **Impact:** You own the 2–3 year technical strategy for a significant portion of the company connected to its competitive position; your decisions have multi-year consequences — the cost of being wrong is measured in years and headcount, not sprints.
- **Communication:** You represent technical constraints and opportunities at the executive level; your communication shapes how leadership allocates capital.

## L10 — Technical leader across the organization, amplifying the organizational architecture

You create the conditions under which great engineering is possible across the company. Your work is systemic and durable.

- **Engineering competency:** You've built the hiring bars, culture, and practices that make strong engineering reproducible and not dependent on any individual; you're accountable for the overall technical health of the company.
- **Influence:** You define what engineering excellence means at this company; your presence shapes technical culture even when you're not in the room.
- **Mentorship/coaching:** You're developing the next generation of L8–L9 technical leaders; the company's technical leadership pipeline is your explicit responsibility.
- **Impact:** You set the technical strategy that anchors the company's multi-year bets; you're accountable for the technical debt that limits what the company can execute on.
- **Communication:** You represent the company's technical credibility externally — to customers, investors, and talent; your writing and speaking shape the company's technical identity in the market.

## On Feedback

Written feedback is the mechanism that makes this framework real. Your manager should help you collect and reflect on feedback — but the documentation is what creates accountability and enables iteration. Verbal feedback evaporates. Written feedback compounds.

When PIPs are appropriate, how to structure them, and what to realistically expect.

A Performance Improvement Plan is a structured intervention for when informal coaching hasn't resolved a documented performance gap. Done well, it's a genuine attempt to help someone succeed. Done poorly, it's a paper trail to an exit.

**HR involvement is mandatory before initiating a PIP.** This is not optional.

## Prerequisites

Before starting a PIP, you must be able to answer yes to all of the following:

- Have you provided **specific, written feedback** about the performance gap — not just verbal conversations?
- Has the employee had **adequate time and support** to address the feedback?
- Have you **ruled out systemic factors** — unclear expectations, under-resourcing, team dynamics — that you own as the manager?
- Have you already had a direct conversation making clear that continued gaps will lead to a formal process?

If any of these are no, the PIP is premature.

## When PIPs Are Appropriate

Use a PIP when there are persistent, documented performance gaps over weeks or months, with coaching already attempted and ineffective.

**Do not use a PIP as:**
- A first response to a performance concern
- A predetermined exit strategy — if you've already decided to let someone go, do that
- A substitute for a direct "this role may not be the right fit" conversation

## Required Structure

An effective PIP must include:

1. **Specific performance gaps** anchored to observable behavior — not "attitude problems" or vague quality concerns.
2. **Concrete success metrics** that are unambiguous (e.g., "Blockers are surfaced to the team within 24 hours of identification").
3. **Manager support commitments** — weekly check-ins, documented, with clear agenda.
4. **Clear timeline** — typically 30 to 90 days depending on the nature of the gap.
5. **Explicit outcomes** — what happens at successful completion, and what happens if the criteria aren't met.

## Running the PIP

The initial conversation must happen in person. Be direct about what's at stake. The employee should leave the meeting with no ambiguity about the seriousness of the situation.

During the PIP, weekly check-ins are not optional. Document everything. Acknowledge genuine progress when it happens — a PIP is not a gotcha exercise. Address any material changes to the person's role or environment immediately, as they affect the fairness of the evaluation.

## Realistic Expectations

Most PIPs do not result in sustained improvement. That is the reality. When they do, it's often because the performance gap was driven by a mismatch in expectations rather than capability or motivation.

If you are managing multiple simultaneous PIPs, that is a signal about broader organizational or management issues — not just individual performance.

A Performance Improvement Plan is a structured intervention for when informal coaching hasn’t resolved a documented performance gap. Done well, it’s a genuine attempt to help someone succeed. Done poorly, it’s a paper trail to an exit.

HR involvement is mandatory before initiating a PIP. This is not optional.

Prerequisites

Before starting a PIP, you must be able to answer yes to all of the following:

  • Have you provided specific, written feedback about the performance gap — not just verbal conversations?
  • Has the employee had adequate time and support to address the feedback?
  • Have you ruled out systemic factors — unclear expectations, under-resourcing, team dynamics — that you own as the manager?
  • Have you already had a direct conversation making clear that continued gaps will lead to a formal process?

If any of these are no, the PIP is premature.

When PIPs Are Appropriate

Use a PIP when there are persistent, documented performance gaps over weeks or months, with coaching already attempted and ineffective.

Do not use a PIP as:

  • A first response to a performance concern
  • A predetermined exit strategy — if you’ve already decided to let someone go, do that
  • A substitute for a direct “this role may not be the right fit” conversation

Required Structure

An effective PIP must include:

  1. Specific performance gaps anchored to observable behavior — not “attitude problems” or vague quality concerns.
  2. Concrete success metrics that are unambiguous (e.g., “Blockers are surfaced to the team within 24 hours of identification”).
  3. Manager support commitments — weekly check-ins, documented, with clear agenda.
  4. Clear timeline — typically 30 to 90 days depending on the nature of the gap.
  5. Explicit outcomes — what happens at successful completion, and what happens if the criteria aren’t met.

Running the PIP

The initial conversation must happen in person. Be direct about what’s at stake. The employee should leave the meeting with no ambiguity about the seriousness of the situation.

During the PIP, weekly check-ins are not optional. Document everything. Acknowledge genuine progress when it happens — a PIP is not a gotcha exercise. Address any material changes to the person’s role or environment immediately, as they affect the fairness of the evaluation.

Realistic Expectations

Most PIPs do not result in sustained improvement. That is the reality. When they do, it’s often because the performance gap was driven by a mismatch in expectations rather than capability or motivation.

If you are managing multiple simultaneous PIPs, that is a signal about broader organizational or management issues — not just individual performance.
How to write a self-review that focuses on impact rather than activity, and actually influences your career trajectory.

A self-review is an imperfect tool. It's not a full picture of your value, and it's not the only input your manager will use. But done well, it gives you the best possible chance of being seen accurately.

## The Core Principle: Impact Over Activity

The most common mistake is writing a list of things you did. Nobody needs that list — your manager was there. What they need to understand is **what changed because you were there**.

Instead of: *"I completed 23 story cards and participated in 4 architectural reviews."*

Write: *"I led the migration to the new auth service, which unblocked the mobile team's Q3 release and reduced login latency by 40%. Two teammates cited my documentation as the reason they were able to onboard in under a week."*

## Structure

**1. Impact Analysis with Peer Feedback**

Lead with your biggest contributions, anchored to outcomes. Support each with specific feedback from colleagues — ideally feedback they wrote independently, not responses to leading questions.

**2. Top 3 Growth Areas**

Be honest. Name the things you're still developing. Informed by colleague observations, not just self-reflection. Showing self-awareness is a signal of seniority, not weakness.

**3. Underutilized Strengths**

What do you bring that the team isn't fully leveraging? This is your chance to shape how your manager thinks about your next quarter.

## Pitfalls to Avoid

- **Self-rating scales** — they tell your reviewer almost nothing and anchor the conversation in the wrong place.
- **Activity lists without outcomes** — nobody cares how many PRs you merged.
- **Vague claims** — "I improved team morale" is not a claim you can defend. "Three teammates mentioned in their own reviews that my technical mentorship improved their confidence on the new stack" is.
- **Overlong reviews** — aim for two pages maximum. Brevity signals clarity of thought.

## On "Exceeding Expectations"

"Exceeding" doesn't mean doing more things. It means delivering consistent, differentiated value that is characteristic of a more senior role. If you want to be promoted to SE3, your review should demonstrate that you're already operating at SE3.

## Practical Tips

- Collect peer feedback actively throughout the year, not just in November.
- Ask follow-up questions when feedback is vague — "Can you give me a specific example?" is always appropriate.
- AI tools can help you sharpen language, but don't let them hallucinate your accomplishments.

*This process does not define your value as a person. It's an imperfect employment validation tool. Approach it strategically, not existentially.*

An interview workflow for evaluating software engineers

![interview workflow](/images/playbook/engineer_workflow.png)

## Stages

### 1. Recruiter Screen

Focus: communication clarity, behavioral signals, and role comprehension.

- Can the candidate articulate what they're looking for and why this role fits?
- Are there any obvious misalignments on level, scope, or expectations?
- Does the candidate understand what the team does?

### 2. Hiring Manager Screen

Focus: technical principles, design pattern familiarity, and architectural reasoning.

1. Explain the outline of the interview and introduce yourself 
2. Have the candidate provide a brief intro and tell me why you want the role.
3. Ask about experiences for "interview-able" [expectations](../career-management/expectations.md). 
  * Look for specific examples and enough context for follow up questions. This helps ensure they will be successful in future interviews.
4. Open it up for questions for the candidate. 

### 3. Pair Programming 

The pair programming session is the most signal-rich part of the process. The goal is not to watch someone solve a puzzle it's to see how they think, communicate, and respond to feedback in a collaborative setting.


### 4. Technical Interview


### 5. Behavioral/Fit Interview


### 6. (optional) Follow up interview with hiring manager


### 7. Offer

2. Hiring Manager Screen

  1. Explain the outline of the interview and introduce yourself
  2. Have the candidate provide a brief intro and tell me why you want the role.
  3. Ask about experiences for “interview-able” expectations.

4. Technical Interview

5. Behavioral/Fit Interview

6. (optional) Follow up interview with hiring manager

7. Offer

Cadence and agenda guidance for 1:1s with developers, tech leads, and staff engineers.

Frequency depends on the individual — every 1 to 3 weeks is a reasonable range. Some people need more frequent touchpoints; others operate well with less. Calibrate based on what you're observing, not just what's scheduled.

Frame feedback in the context of [level expectations](/playbook/career-management/expectations) and technical leadership expectations so conversations about growth are anchored to something concrete.

## Developer 1:1

- Team member's agenda first — always
- Review team meeting contributions
- Review task execution (story cards, blockers, pace)
- Collect and review peer feedback
- Side quests — things they're thinking about outside their assigned work

## Tech Lead 1:1

- Team member's agenda first
- Delivery concerns — what's at risk, what needs unblocking
- People concerns — team dynamics, interpersonal friction
- Technical and architecture concerns
- Review priorities and alignment with broader roadmap

## Staff+ 1:1

- Team member's agenda first
- Delivery concerns
- People concerns
- Technical and architecture concerns
- Organizational and system challenges — cross-team friction, structural issues, process gaps
- Review priorities

Frame feedback in the context of level expectations and technical leadership expectations so conversations about growth are anchored to something concrete.

  • Technical and architecture concerns
  • Organizational and system challenges — cross-team friction, structural issues, process gaps
  • Review priorities
A structured template for incident retrospectives, from detection through action items.

## Header

```
time-of-detection:
time-of-acknowledgement:
time-of-recovery:
```

## Executive Summary

*Write a summary of the incident that includes "what happened", "measured impact", and "recovery". Brevity and precision are important.*

## Impact

*Explain the impact to customers — internal or external — using specific numbers or calculated estimates.*

## Timeline

*Write a timeline of events starting at detection. Capture any relevant events that might provide insight into root cause or be worth retrospecting on. We want to learn from this event log.*

```
<date> - <CT time>: <Significant moment for detection, impact to customers, recovery, etc.>
```

## Root Cause Analysis

*Describe the root cause of the failure that affects this team's system. The heart of the issue could live with another team. Reference another post-mortem if an error occurs at an interface boundary.*

## Retrospective

*Run a 30 to 60 minute retrospective. Cover "what went well", "what did not", and "where we got lucky". Focus discussion on a shared understanding of the latter two to generate action items. Include a link to the facilitation resources and recording.*

## Action Items

*List action items from the retrospective. Links to story cards are preferable.*

time-of-acknowledgement:
time-of-recovery:

A standard template for defining work scope, acceptance criteria, and technical context.

## Summary

*What is the expected output or value delivered by this card? One or two sentences.*

## Background

*Optional. What context or rationale is useful for someone picking this up cold? Link to relevant discussions, decisions, or prior work.*

## Acceptance Criteria

*Testable conditions for completion. Use given/when/then structure where it helps clarity.*

- Given [context], when [action], then [outcome]

## Tech Notes

*Credential locations, relevant documentation, known challenges, architectural constraints, or anything that would otherwise require a conversation before getting started.*

Summary

What is the expected output or value delivered by this card? One or two sentences.

Background

Optional. What context or rationale is useful for someone picking this up cold? Link to relevant discussions, decisions, or prior work.

Acceptance Criteria

Testable conditions for completion. Use given/when/then structure where it helps clarity.

  • Given [context], when [action], then [outcome]

Tech Notes

Credential locations, relevant documentation, known challenges, architectural constraints, or anything that would otherwise require a conversation before getting started.
A living document template for team ceremonies, workflow stages, and operating norms.

*This is a template. Copy and adapt it for your team. It should live somewhere everyone can edit it.*

---
draft: true

## Ceremonies

### Standup — 25 minutes, daily

Keep it short. The goal is shared context on progress and blockers, not status reporting to management.

- **10 min** — Updates and blockers on active work, plus any support needed
- **5 min** — Logistics, upcoming events, team announcements
- **10 min** — Parking lot for anything that needs a quick follow-up

### Retro — 70 minutes, every 3 weeks

Celebrate what's working. Surface friction before it becomes dysfunction.

- Wins and shoutouts
- Roadmap and priority review
- Retrospective cards (what went well, what didn't, what to try)
- Action item assignment

Use a facilitation tool (Mural, FunRetro, etc.) and rotate the facilitator.

### Tech Huddle — 45 minutes, weekly

Technical knowledge sharing, architectural discussions, and communication skill development. Pre-registered topics only — no ad hoc agenda items. Assign a facilitator for the week.

### Engineering Kickoffs — as needed

Run when new work begins. Present problem context, architectural overview, and story map. Prefer in-person or synchronous video.

---
draft: true

## Flow of Work

| Stage | Description |
|---|---|
| Analysis | Work is being scoped and broken down |
| Ready for Dev | 15-minute kickoff done, acceptance criteria clear |
| In Progress | Active development |
| Done | Work complete, deskcheck requested |
| Released | Deployed to production, deskcheck confirmed |

**Spikes** are timeboxed research cards. Define the question you're answering and the timebox upfront. The output is a decision or a recommendation, not code.

---
draft: true

## Core Practices

**Pair Programming** — The default working modality. Reduces knowledge silos. Uses an anchor rotation model (see [Pairing Guidance](/playbook/technical-leadership/pairing-guidance)).

**Documentation** — Maintained continuously, not in a crunch before a release. Includes architecture diagrams, API specs, event schemas, and operational runbooks.

**Support Rotation** — Weekly rotation for first-line team support. One person is the designated responder so the rest of the team can stay focused.

Core Practices

Pair Programming — The default working modality. Reduces knowledge silos. Uses an anchor rotation model (see Pairing Guidance).

Documentation — Maintained continuously, not in a crunch before a release. Includes architecture diagrams, API specs, event schemas, and operational runbooks.

Ten engineering organization defaults that raise the baseline for how teams build and ship software.

*Inspired by [Thoughtworks Sensible Defaults](https://www.thoughtworks.com/en-us/insights/topic/sensible-defaults).*

These are not rules. They're defaults — starting positions that should be followed unless you have a good reason not to, and where exceptions require explicit justification.

1. **Integrate frequently and continuously** — If you're not integrating at least daily, you're accumulating merge risk.
2. **Code is written with test-driven development** — Tests aren't written after; they're written first. This changes how you design, not just how you verify.
3. **Develop in pairs** — See [Pairing Guidance](/playbook/technical-leadership/pairing-guidance).
4. **Smallest value-oriented stories** — Break work down until each card can be delivered and validated independently. Large cards hide risk.
5. **Security is automated in the SDLC** — Security checks run in the pipeline, not as a quarterly audit.
6. **Every code change is monitored and observable** — If you can't observe the behavior of your code in production, you don't know if it works.
7. **Build pipelines automate local development** — The pipeline should remove friction from running the system locally, not just from CI.
8. **Technical change requires a value proposition** — Refactors, upgrades, and rewrites need to be justified in terms of value delivered, not just technical preference.
9. **Deployment is decoupled from release** — You can deploy any time. Whether users see the change is controlled separately, via feature flags or equivalent.
10. **Measure quality** — Code coverage, static analysis, and other quality signals are tracked and visible.

## Supporting Resources

- [Continuous Integration](https://martinfowler.com/articles/continuousIntegration.html) — Martin Fowler
- [Test-Driven Development](https://martinfowler.com/bliki/TestDrivenDevelopment.html) — Martin Fowler
- [On Pair Programming](https://martinfowler.com/articles/on-pair-programming.html) — Martin Fowler
- [Feature Toggles](https://martinfowler.com/articles/feature-toggles.html) — Martin Fowler
- [Observability-Driven Development](https://www.honeycomb.io/blog/observability-driven-development) — Honeycomb
- [Snyk CI/CD Integration](https://docs.snyk.io/scm-ide-and-ci-cd-integrations/snyk-ci-cd-integrations/snyk-ci-cd-integration-deployment-and-strategies)

These are not rules. They’re defaults — starting positions that should be followed unless you have a good reason not to, and where exceptions require explicit justification.

  1. Integrate frequently and continuously — If you’re not integrating at least daily, you’re accumulating merge risk.
  2. Code is written with test-driven development — Tests aren’t written after; they’re written first. This changes how you design, not just how you verify.
  3. Develop in pairs — See Pairing Guidance.
  4. Smallest value-oriented stories — Break work down until each card can be delivered and validated independently. Large cards hide risk.
  5. Security is automated in the SDLC — Security checks run in the pipeline, not as a quarterly audit.
  6. Every code change is monitored and observable — If you can’t observe the behavior of your code in production, you don’t know if it works.
  7. Build pipelines automate local development — The pipeline should remove friction from running the system locally, not just from CI.
  8. Technical change requires a value proposition — Refactors, upgrades, and rewrites need to be justified in terms of value delivered, not just technical preference.
  9. Deployment is decoupled from release — You can deploy any time. Whether users see the change is controlled separately, via feature flags or equivalent.
  10. Measure quality — Code coverage, static analysis, and other quality signals are tracked and visible.
